data.title: ‘Cheap for a Reason,’ Says Investor About Microsoft Stock
data.description:
It looks like another round of layoffs is imminent at Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT). According to a Tuesday report from Business Insider, citing sources, the tech giant is planning to reduce its workforce by under 2.5%, with an announcement potentially coming as early as next week.

End of Quarter Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock powerful investing tools and data-driven insights with TipRanks Premium for more confident investment decisions.
  • Discover top stock picks and new investment opportunities through TipRanks' Smart Investor Newsletter.

The cuts could impact thousands of employees across Microsoft’s 220,000-strong workforce, with roles in sales, consulting, and Xbox among those potentially affected. In prior years, Microsoft has often implemented job reductions around the beginning of its fiscal year on July 1. Last year, it cut 6,000 roles in May and then removed a further 9,000 positions in July, amounting to roughly 4% of its workforce.

The planned layoffs highlight Microsoft’s broader effort to control costs while significantly increasing investment in artificial intelligence. The company has also faced investor concerns that AI could disrupt software makers, including parts of Microsoft’s own product suite. MSFT shares have fallen about 19% over the past month, marking the stock’s weakest monthly performance since the dot-com days.

However, rather than the depressed share price offering a real opportunity, investor Vladimir Dimitrov thinks skepticism is merited here. “Deterioration in investors’ sentiment towards Microsoft and hyperscalers more broadly is justified given the current level of uncertainty,” says Dimitrov.

Despite historically strong margins that once helped support the stock “through all kinds of weather,” Microsoft is now facing pressure on profitability. The strain is largely driven by a heavy AI infrastructure buildout and rising capital expenditures. The development and operation of AI-focused data centers has increased cost of revenue and led to noticeable gross margin compression within the high-growth Azure cloud business. For instance, Intelligent Cloud segment margins for the first nine months of fiscal year 2026 are lower than those reported a year earlier.

Looking ahead, Dimitrov says the uncertainty around margins and returns on large capital investments significantly widens the range of possible outcomes for FY 2027. As a result, the stock is now trading at a notable discount to its current underlying business fundamentals.

At the same time, overall uncertainty in the cloud industry has risen sharply in recent months amid intensifying competition and the elevated spending levels. The AI investment race could leave hyperscalers in a position where they must deploy vast amounts of capital without clear visibility into future returns. “All that creates significant uncertainty going forward, which, for the time being, justifies MSFT trading at a discount relative to its current margins,” Dimitrov summed up.

To this end, Dimitrov rates MSFT stock a Hold (i.e., Neutral). (To watch Dimitrov’s track record, click here)

Barring one analyst who also remains on the sidelines, all 35 other recent MSFT stock reviews are positive, making the consensus view a Strong Buy. At $562.10, the average price target offers 12-month upside of 51%. (See MSFT stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Armageddon Called Off: Guggenheim Upgrades Software Stocks Salesforce and ServiceNow to Buy – Here’s Why
data.description:
Software stocks have been subjected to heavy beatings this year, largely driven by an investor selloff dubbed the “SaaSpocalypse.” The concern is that advanced AI agents and large language models could eventually replace traditional subscription-based software, weakening pricing power and compressing margins.

End of Quarter Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock powerful investing tools and data-driven insights with TipRanks Premium for more confident investment decisions.
  • Discover top stock picks and new investment opportunities through TipRanks' Smart Investor Newsletter.

As a result, segment giants such as Salesforce (NYSE:CRM) and ServiceNow (NYSE:NOW) have seen their share prices collapse. The former has shed 41% year-to-date while the latter is down by 35%.

Now, however, with the valuations having significantly contracted, Guggenheim’s John DiFucci, an analyst ranked among the top 3% on Wall Street, thinks the investment case for both has become far more appealing.

For Salesforce, he argues its current valuation of 3.7x recurring revenue and 11x EV/NTM consensus FCF “presents an attractive entry point for investors.”

While DiFucci acknowledges AI is a “significant risk” and expects Salesforce to be negatively affected by the rise of agentic AI, he believes the market is pricing in an overly extreme “Armageddon” outcome that is not realistic. Instead, he models a scenario where Salesforce sees limited growth rather than meaningful decline, whereas the current share price implies it “declines 5% into perpetuity.”

“To be clear,” the 5-star analyst stresses, “this is not a call that CRM will be a beneficiary of AI, but we don’t believe it will decline as implied in the current valuation.”

As such, DiFucci upgraded his CRM rating from Neutral to Buy and set a $228 price target, a figure that suggests upside of 45.5% from current levels. (To watch DiFucci’s track record, click here)

The Street’s average target is actually higher, landing at $245.51, and implying shares will gain 57% in the year ahead. All told, based on 28 Buys, 6 Holds, and 2 Sells, the analyst consensus rates the stock a Moderate Buy.  (See CRM stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Looking for a ‘Physical AI Pure Play’? Ambarella Stock Is the One, Says Rosenblatt
data.description:
Physical AI is often touted as the next big thing. Going beyond text-based chatbots, it integrates intelligence into robots and machines, allowing them to perceive, adapt to, and operate within the physical environment.

End of Quarter Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock powerful investing tools and data-driven insights with TipRanks Premium for more confident investment decisions.
  • Discover top stock picks and new investment opportunities through TipRanks' Smart Investor Newsletter.

With AI stocks driving the ongoing bull market, investors are obviously looking to gain an edge here, and fittingly, edge AI chipmaker Ambarella (NASDAQ:AMBA) might just be the place to find it.

At least that is the opinion of Rosenblatt’s Kevin Cassidy, who ranks among the top 1% of Street stock experts. Cassidy helped set off a 28% rally in AMBA shares on Tuesday, after singling out this name as his “top pick” for the second half of the year.

Cassidysees Physical AI as the “next stage of growth in the Age of AI,” and considers AMBA a “Physical AI pure play.”

“Applications such as surveillance, robotics, industrial automation, drones and autonomous systems require high-performance, low-power AI vision processors close to the sensor,” the 5-star analyst explained. “Ambarella’s algorithm first AI SoC architecture delivers roughly 50 TOPS to 500 TOPS of performance at less than 5 watts. These are necessary performance/watt specifications to run billion parameter models at the network edge.”

Cassidy points out that Ambarella has kept up with leading-edge semiconductor process nodes, with 5nm Samsung SoCs already in volume production, 4nm Samsung CV7 expected to ramp next year, and a 2nm CV8 targeted for 2QF28 for network-edge use cases across consumer and enterprise markets. The company has also secured a long-term deal to build a 2nm semi-custom ASIC tailored to a customer’s complex AI workloads, with management targeting at least approximately $100 million in revenue from custom and semi-custom SoCs.

Meanwhile, rising costs of advanced process wafers are pushing more companies to reconsider in-house SoC development, a trend that Cassidy thinks is beneficial for Ambarella. Early evidence of this shift includes a long-term agreement worth over $800 million with Korean conglomerate Hanwha, spanning more than 10 years. Cassidy expects the arrangement to begin contributing revenue upside next year as Hanwha transitions fully from a mix of internal and external SoC sourcing to Ambarella supplying all of its security camera chips, potentially adding about $50 million in that segment alone, with further upside from expansion into Hanwha’s broader businesses such as healthcare, robotics, drones, automation, and life sciences.

“We believe this validates that AI design complexity and advanced-node costs are driving customers back toward merchant AI SoC vendors, a major change from the prior cycle that we think the market is still underappreciating the significance of this change,” the analyst further said.

Among its strengths, Cassidy highlights Ambarella’s strong edge AI product cycle, rising AI SoC average selling prices, a new semi-custom/long-term agreement growth driver, and its positioning as “one of the few public semiconductor companies directly levered to Physical AI at the edge.”

To this end, Cassidy reiterated a Buy rating on AMBA shares along with a $120 price target, suggesting the stock will gain another 40% in the months ahead. (To watch Cassidy’s track record, click here)

Elsewhere on the Street, AMBA stock claims an additional 6 Buys and 4 Holds, for a Moderate Buy consensus rating. The average target stands at $101.56, implying the stock will appreciate by 18% over the next year. (See Ambarella stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘Time for Action’: Circle Stock on Sale, Says William Blair
data.description:
A new stablecoin entrant is shaking up the market, and investors have reacted by selling shares of an established player. On Tuesday, Circle Internet (NYSE:CRCL) stock dropped 17.5% after a new consortium revealed plans to introduce a rival stablecoin, a digital cryptocurrency designed to maintain a stable value by being pegged to an asset such as the U.S. dollar.

End of Quarter Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock powerful investing tools and data-driven insights with TipRanks Premium for more confident investment decisions.
  • Discover top stock picks and new investment opportunities through TipRanks' Smart Investor Newsletter.

At present, the stablecoin market is dominated by two names: Tether’s USDT and Circle’s USDC, which rank first and second by market cap. USDC is the only stablecoin issued by a publicly traded firm.

However, a coalition of more than 140 companies, including Visa, Mastercard, Stripe, and BlackRock, announced the formation of an independent organization called Open Standard to develop a competing U.S. dollar-pegged stablecoin, Open USD (OUSD). The new token is intended to address persistent cost and accessibility challenges in global payments and is scheduled to launch later in 2026.

The announcement weighed heavily on Circle because OUSD is aimed at the same enterprise customers that underpin USDC’s ecosystem. Adding to investor concerns, Coinbase, Circle’s most important partner, is also participating in the initiative. Circle paid Coinbase over $900 million in 2024 to distribute USDC, but under the new framework, coalition members will retain the yield generated by reserve assets. That change has fueled concerns about potential disruption to Circle’s existing distribution model and revenue streams, given that roughly 99% of its revenue is derived from the interest generated by USDC reserves.

So, are investors right to be concerned here? Not really, says William Blair analyst Andrew Jeffrey, who thinks investors could do well by picking up shares at a discount. “We think investors should add CRCL as the stock overreacts to competitive fears spurred by the announced launch of a competing stablecoin from Open Standard, dubbed Open USD,” the 5-star analyst said.

In Jeffrey’s view, competition in the more than $20 trillion stablecoin market is unavoidable and serves as further validation of the commercial opportunity for Circle and USDC. The analyst believes investor concerns about new rivals are “overblown,” citing USDC’s significant first-mover advantage, stronger liquidity, and CPN (Circle Payments Network), its stablecoin movement infrastructure. Jeffrey also argues that replicating Circle’s business model will be difficult for new entrants and that features such as sharing reserve income are not meaningfully differentiated. “We see Open USD as a solution searching for a problem,” he summed up.

To this end, Jeffrey maintained an Outperform (i.e., Buy) rating on the shares, without having a fixed price target in mind. (To watch Jeffrey’s track record, click here)

Others on the Street do have targets and the average lands at $134.13, implying the stock will gain a hefty 114% in the months ahead. On the rating front, based on a mix of 9 Buys, 6 Holds and 2 Sells, the analyst consensus views this stock as a Moderate Buy. (See CRCL stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘Watch Out SpaceX’: Why Analysts Are Raising Rocket Lab Stock’s Price Target Today
data.description:
Sentiment is fickle on Wall Street, something Rocket Lab (NASDAQ:RKLB) investors are currently keenly aware of.

End of Quarter Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock powerful investing tools and data-driven insights with TipRanks Premium for more confident investment decisions.
  • Discover top stock picks and new investment opportunities through TipRanks' Smart Investor Newsletter.

The space company’s shares have been very volatile, recently falling from a May 2026 all-time peak of $151, all the way down to the low $80s, the sell-off driven by the SpaceX IPO, before rebounding toward $100, with Monday’s 16% gain particularly eye-catching.

The uptick was driven by the company’s announcement that it is set to acquire Iridium Communications, a major provider of satellite communications and PNT services. This accelerates the firm’s push into space applications and will help build a vertically integrated space platform spanning launch, spacecraft manufacturing, spectrum, and on-orbit communications services.

The transaction values Iridium at $54 per share, implying a total enterprise value of $8 billion, representing a 24% premium to last Friday’s closing price.  

Iridium delivered revenue of $872 million and a 57% adjusted EBITDA margin in 2025, suggesting the deal will roughly double Rocket Lab’s total revenue and leave the combined business EBITDA-positive. The transaction is anticipated to close in mid-2027.

So, investors evidently liked the news and so did BofA’s Ronald Epstein, an analyst ranked among the top 2% on Wall Street. “RKLB has discussed entry into the space applications market for years as a means of monetizing its existing capabilities in launch and space systems, and we see this acquisition as a favorable entry into key commercial, government, and defense services markets,” the 5-star analyst said.  

Although Iridium’s current constellation network could continue supporting operations, the analyst expects the acquisition to raise internal demand for Neutron launches to sustain the network over time. Although Neutron capacity is largely committed through 2028, having an internal use case for the vehicle could “drive higher cadences” and strengthen third-party pricing from 2029 and beyond.

Rocket Lab has historically used M&A and in-house R&D to advance vertical integration, including satellite payloads (notably via the Geost and Mynaric acquisitions and development of its Gauss Hall effect thruster), suggesting the company could pursue antenna and signal-processing capabilities through acquisitions or internal development.

To this end, Epstein reiterated a Buy rating on the shares and raised his price objective from $105 to $115, implying the stock will climb 19% higher over the coming months. “The PO change reflects elevated appetite by RKLB to acquire higher-margin space services opportunities in our bull case, reflected in higher revenue and adj. EBITDA margin expectations from 2027E,” Epstein further added. (To watch Epstein’s track record, click here)

Citizens’ Trevor Walsh, an analyst ranked among the top 3% on the Street, also sees the deal in a positive light. At a “high level,” the analyst views the acquisition as addressing a missing component management has pointed out for years in Rocket Lab’s end-to-end space vision, adding an owned services layer to its launch and spacecraft manufacturing base.

“While the broader strategy remains in the earlier stages and still requires deal approval, we view the proposed acquisition as a major milestone in the company’s transition from a space infrastructure provider toward a vertically integrated space operator,” the 5-star analyst explained. “The team continues to execute on all fronts, and we view Rocket Lab as an opportunity for long-term capital appreciation.”

Accordingly, Walsh maintained a Market Outperform (i.e., Buy) rating on the shares, and raised his price target from $95 to $130, suggesting the stock will gain 35% in the months ahead. (To watch Walsh’s track record, click here)

Elsewhere on the Street, the shares claim an additional 10 Buys and 3 Holds, for a Strong Buy consensus view. At $111.38, the average price target factors in 12-month share appreciation of 15.5%. (See RKLB stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Why AeroVironment Stock Is Surging Today, and Why Stifel Just Lowered Its Price Target
data.description:
Tuesday is shaping up to be an excellent day for AeroVironment (NASDAQ:AVAV) investors. The shares are up by 21% as of writing, after the defense technology firm’s fiscal fourth-quarter results exceeded Wall Street expectations and delivered record quarterly revenue.

End of Quarter Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock powerful investing tools and data-driven insights with TipRanks Premium for more confident investment decisions.
  • Discover top stock picks and new investment opportunities through TipRanks' Smart Investor Newsletter.

The rally is a welcome one, and comes after a prolonged weak period for the stock. Prior to Monday’s readout, AVAV shares had declined by ~51% over the past year.

For the April quarter, AeroVironment posted revenue of $641.6 million, representing a 133% year-over-year increase and above the Street’s call of $559 million. Adjusted EPS came in at $1.84, beating the consensus estimate of $1.47.

Looking past the headline figures, another key metric was the company’s backlog, which had risen from less than $727 million during the same period in 2025 to $1.2 billion.

A significant portion of the quarterly growth was driven by AeroVironment’s acquisitions of BlueHalo and Empirical Systems Aerospace, which together contributed revenue of $282.3 million.

Looking ahead, even a miss in the forecast failed to dampen investor enthusiasm. For fiscal 2027, AeroVironment guided for adj. EPS between $3.02 and $3.34, below the $4.00 analysts were looking for. The revenue guide of $2.125 billion to $2.225 billion was broadly in line with the $2.18 billion midpoint anticipated on Wall Street.

Scanning the results, Stifel analyst Jonathan Siegmann said the strong quarterly performance combined with a softer outlook was the inverse of his expectations heading into the print. “While ~10% guided revenue growth may seem underwhelming at first, the FY27 outlook includes a ~5% drag from the canceled SCAR program and similar sized headwind from roll-off of Ukraine sales,” the analyst pointed out.

AVAV is also assuming that completion of the FY27 defense budget will be pushed back until the end of the year.

With investor expectations “likely beaten down” and valuation sitting at ~20x FY2027 EBITDA, the analyst views AVAV shares as “attractively valued.” He shares management’s optimism around near-term directed energy awards and expects franchise awards to act as a potential catalyst for the stock.

That said, Siegmann has made some downward revisions to his model, with FY27 EBITDA reduced to reflect AVAV’s “lower-than-expected margins and guidance.”

“Given the headwinds in AVAV’s portfolio (specifically the SCAR canceled contract), muted margin improvement, and the general dour market mood across defense stocks, we now think it is too optimistic to expect the market to anchor on estimates beyond next year,” Siegmann further said up.

So, while Siegmann maintained a Buy rating on the shares, he also lowered his price target from $315 to $220, although the new figure still makes room for 12-month returns of 31%. (To watch Siegmann’s track record, click here)

The Street’s average target is only a tad lower; at $216.57, the shares are expected to post 12-month growth of 29%. All told, the stock claims a Strong Buy consensus rating, based on 7 Buys and 1 Hold. (See AVAV stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Why Rocket Lab Stock Is Surging Today and Why This Investor Calls It a Strong Sell
data.description:
After a period in the doldrums, Rocket Lab (NASDAQ:RKLB) appears to be picking up steam once again. The shares are up by 10% in Monday’s session, the uptick driven by news of the space company’s latest acquisition.

End of Quarter Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock powerful investing tools and data-driven insights with TipRanks Premium for more confident investment decisions.
  • Discover top stock picks and new investment opportunities through TipRanks' Smart Investor Newsletter.

Rocket Lab has agreed to buy Iridium Communications through a cash-and-stock transaction worth approximately $8 billion. Iridium Communications runs a global satellite communications network that delivers voice, data, and IoT connectivity worldwide through a constellation of low-Earth orbit satellites. According to Rocket Lab, the acquisition will bring together its launch and satellite manufacturing operations with Iridium’s global satellite communications network and L-band spectrum, thereby forming a vertically integrated space business.

Rocket Lab said the acquisition is expected to be immediately transformative by contributing recurring cash flow and profitability. In 2025, Iridium reported $871.7 million in revenue and $495 million in OEBITDA, amounting to a 57% margin.

Investors evidently like the sound of the deal, but will it change the stance of a new RKLB bear, an investor who goes by the name of Bears of Wall Street (BWS)?

BWS says the bear case has “never been stronger,” first pointing out that the two major catalysts that have helped push the stock higher – SpaceX’s blockbuster IPO and Rocket Lab’s addition to the Nasdaq-100 – are now behind us.  

Maybe that doesn’t matter much now a new catalyst has just come into play, but nevertheless, BWS thinks RKLB’s valuation makes no sense, with the stock trading at around 60 times its forward sales. “Considering that Rocket Lab is not expected to generate a profit until around 2028 and that it is still losing money with a net loss of about $45 million in the first quarter, we believe that paying such a price for a company that won’t generate earnings for a while is difficult to justify,” BWS explained.

A further concern for Rocket Lab is that a big chunk of the “bullish thesis” depends on a rocket that has yet to reach space. The timetable for its reusable Neutron rocket has been pushed back to late 2026, the postponement partly caused by a rupture in the Stage 1 tank during a test conducted earlier this year. Even though schedule delays are typical within the space sector, there is no assurance that Neutron’s launch will not be postponed again. Meanwhile, management has projected higher expenditures for this year. “Because of that, we have a situation where the money is already leaving the company at a time when not a single Neutron rocket has been launched yet,” the investor added.

Lastly, another challenge for Rocket Lab is its dependence on external financing to operate the business, driven by unprofitability and negative free cash flow. That is not inherently problematic, but it implies that existing shareholders “continue to get diluted over time,” which becomes a meaningful cost when the stock is “already priced for perfection.”

Put all that together and BWS initiated coverage of RKLB stock with a Strong Sell rating. (To watch Bears of Wall Street’s track record, click here)

However, that take gets no support on Wall Street. In fact, quite the opposite. The stock is considered a Strong Buy, based on 10 Buys and 3 Holds. At $108.70, the average price target offers 12-month upside of 17%. (See RKLB stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘More Upside Ahead’: UBS Highlights Marvell Stock’s CXL Opportunity
data.description:
Marvell (NASDAQ:MRVL) stock has been benefiting from strong data center demand, rapid expansion in optical interconnects, and wins in custom silicon programs with large hyperscale customers.

End of Quarter Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock powerful investing tools and data-driven insights with TipRanks Premium for more confident investment decisions.
  • Discover top stock picks and new investment opportunities through TipRanks' Smart Investor Newsletter.

The company is also a big player in the CXL (Compute Express Link) market. Marvell provides a leading end-to-end CXL fabric architecture intended to address the AI memory bottleneck by enabling large, shared memory pools and accelerating data-heavy workloads.

UBS’s Timothy Arcuri, an analyst who ranks in 2nd spot among the thousands of Street stock experts,believes the “CXL opportunity is inflecting,” and as we move towards the end of the decade, MRVL is one of the companies positioned to benefit. “Cache-coherent, low-latency, high-bandwidth interconnect built on PCIe is expanding the market, and CXL is becoming a critical enabling technology,” the 5-star analyst said.

CXL is essentially like an upgrade to PCIe that lets CPUs and accelerators share memory directly, not just send data back and forth. It helps create a shared memory system across different devices. Both are growing in importance because AI systems need more hardware and much more memory.

Estimates have previously placed the CXL market at around $4.5 billion by C2027, although Arcuri thinks the ultimate TAM (total addressable market) remains uncertain due to differences in attach rates and solution configurations, including extenders and switches that vary by deployment.

However, Arcuri thinks attach rates per XPU/CPU should continue to increase, rising from roughly $100–200 per processor to higher multiples as applications expand from single-CPU memory connections toward coherent rack-level and potentially pod-level fabrics. He estimates that combined XPU, head node CPU, and standalone CPU unit volumes will reach approximately 73 million by C2030, with the CXL market approaching $7–10 billion over that period.

As for Marvell specifically, it has stated that it expects CXL revenue to grow from an approximately $100 million annual run rate to around $1 billion by C2027, with further growth beyond that point.

CXL revenue is divided into three segments: interconnect (legacy memory-expansion use cases), XPU attach (new hyperscaler custom silicon designs, where Marvell has five programs with two major US hyperscalers, and switching (CXL switches).

The company has also indicated that most of the ~$1 billion CXL revenue target is driven by XPU-attach sockets rather than CPU-attach opportunities, with emerging “agentic tailwinds” providing incremental upside to CPU adoption on top of existing XPU-focused programs.

Given increasing expectations for both XPU attach and switching revenues in C2027 and beyond, Arcuri has now raised his MRVL estimates. Overall, the analyst’s revenue forecasts for C2027/C2028 rise to $16.8 billion/$23.9 billion (from $16.5 billion/$21.9 billion), while EPS estimates increase to $6.23/$9.62 (from $6.09/$8.60). The ~$2 billion uplift in C2028 revenue estimates is driven by stronger CXL demand.  

All of that merits a new price target, which rises from $230 to $340, suggesting the stock will gain 27% over the next year. Arcuri’s rating stays a Buy. (To watch Arcuri’s track record, click here)

Elsewhere on the Street, 23 analysts join UBS in the bull camp, while 4 Holds can’t detract from a Strong Buy consensus rating. However, the $266.96 average price target implies the shares are fully valued. That gap likely reflects how quickly the stock has risen (up 214% year-to-date) rather than weakening conviction, which suggests that more upward revisions to price targets may follow soon. (See MRVL stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘September Is Key,’ Says Top Analyst About Apple Stock Following Price Hikes
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) stock appears to be on a more even keel in Friday’s trading, which is certainly an improvement over Thursday’s action. The shares had their worst day in more than a year, falling by 6% after the company upset investors with its latest move.

TipRanks' Prime Week Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock powerful investing tools and data-driven insights with TipRanks Premium for more confident investment decisions.
  • Discover top stock picks and new investment opportunities through TipRanks' Smart Investor Newsletter.

The tech giant increased prices mid-cycle for selected Macs, iPads, and home devices, following recent remarks from Tim Cook about mounting memory supply constraints.

The price increases were applied across much of the core Mac and iPad range, with base-model configurations rising by 17% to 25%, while lower-priced home devices saw even steeper percentage increases, including a 54% jump for Apple TV. The adjustments appear to reflect higher memory costs, with DRAM and NAND prices now several times higher than a year ago and significantly above where they stood at the beginning of the year, when Apple refreshed the iPad Air, iPad Pro, MacBook Air, and MacBook Pro lineups and introduced the MacBook Neo.

Evercore’s Amit Daryanani, an analyst ranked among the top 1% on Wall Street, was taken aback by the move. “The price hikes across the select products come as a surprise, as we were under the impression that Apple would wait until the next product cycle before raising prices,” the 5-star analyst said.

Daryanani also noted that Apple’s long-term memory supply agreements were rolling off during the quarter, which left the company more exposed to spot market pricing. While higher memory prices have already been widely understood, the intra-cycle price increase suggests that the “magnitude and pace” of the cost pressure exceeded Apple’s capacity to absorb it. It is also significant that iPhones were excluded from the price hikes, as Daryanani anticipates Apple will wait until the September iPhone release to increase prices on the new iPhone 18 Pro models.

So, what are the implications for the investment case? The company’s actions indicate that memory price inflation is “biting harder and faster” than anticipated, even for Apple. “While the price increases should help protect gross margins, intra-cycle hikes are very unusual for Apple and raise the risk of some demand friction across Macs and iPads,” the analyst summed up. “The iPhone exclusion keeps the NT impact contained but also sets up September as the next key pricing event.”

For now, Daryanani sticks with his Outperform (i.e., Buy) rating and $365 price target, implying the stock will gain 32% over the one-year timeframe. (To watch Daryanani’s track record, click here)

17 other analysts join Daryanani in the AAPL bull camp, while an additional 11 Holds and 1 Sell add up to a Moderate Buy consensus rating. At $324.40, the average price target offers 12-month upside of 18%. (See Apple stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘A Hidden Quantum Gem’: Why Wedbush Thinks Infleqtion Stock Is Undervalued
data.description:
The quantum computing sector has seen a big wave of companies go public this year, one of those being Infleqtion (NYSE:INFQ), which made its debut in February following a merger with a special purpose acquisition company (SPAC).

TipRanks' Prime Week Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock powerful investing tools and data-driven insights with TipRanks Premium for more confident investment decisions.
  • Discover top stock picks and new investment opportunities through TipRanks' Smart Investor Newsletter.

In this nascent industry, a plethora of companies are vying for attention and taking different approaches, so what makes Infleqtion stand out?

As Wedbush analyst Antoine Legault notes, the company is the only one “commercializing the entire neutral-atom stack across computing, sensing, and software from one shared technology core, and the only publicly traded neutral-atom pure-play.”

Essentially, neutral-atom quantum technology is a way of building a quantum computer using individual atoms that are electrically neutral (meaning they carry no net electric charge). Unlike other quantum computing approaches that use superconducting circuits or electrically charged ions, neutral-atom systems use uncharged individual atoms trapped and controlled with lasers as the qubits.

In Legault’s view, neutral-atom systems have become a credible and competitive modality. Given the absence of agreement on a final modality winner, the analyst sees INFQ as “one of the most compelling ways to gain exposure to the category.”

So, based on the assumption that neutral atoms represent a viable modality, the analyst sees INFQ as “meaningfully mispriced”: it holds the second-largest revenue base in the public quantum cohort yet sits sixth in valuation, trading at a “steep discount” vs. several peers even though they are expected to generate lower revenue.

Legault thinks this mispricing is driven by three factors that are expected to diminish over time. The first is “modality perception.” Neutral atoms are regarded as a newer, less established approach, not yet granted the same credibility as the more mature trapped-ion and superconducting modalities, despite “rapid technical progress.”

Secondly, with about two-thirds of revenue derived from sensing, the market might see INFQ as a sensing-focused company, assigning limited credit to its compute progress or its full-stack platform.

Lastly, the company has been on the market for only a few months and still receives limited sell-side coverage, has lower trading activity relative to peers, factors that can “weigh on valuation independent of fundamentals.”

“As the modality matures, the compute story gains recognition, and liquidity builds, we expect INFQ’s discount to the group to moderate,” Legault opined.

Meanwhile, the roster is supported by an extensive Nvidia collaboration (NVQLink, CUDA-Q, both Ising models) and is further broadened to include Safran, NASA, Voyager, and DARPA. The analyst anticipates further high-profile partnerships, with each expected to function as a “potential catalyst.”

Additionally, a proposed $100 million Department of Commerce CHIPS letter of intent (which considers a potential US government equity stake) and a recent executive order, which mandates the deployment of at least three quantum sensor projects by September 30, 2028, indicate a “clear federal demand pull for exactly what INFQ sells.”

Put all that together and Legault initiated coverage of INFQ with an Outperform (i.e., Buy) rating and $20 price target, a figure pointing toward 12-month returns of 53%. (To watch Legault’s track record, click here)

While only two other analysts have so far waded in with INFQ reviews, both are also positive, making the consensus view a Strong Buy. At $20.67, the average price target offers one-year upside of 58%. (See INFQ stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Micron’s Blowout Earnings Have Changed the Game for Sandisk Stock, Says Top Analyst
data.description:
Micron’s estimate-trouncing fiscal third-quarter results are the big story on Thursday, the report laying to rest concerns around the broader AI trade by reinforcing very strong memory market economics.

TipRanks' Prime Week Sale - 70% Off

Two ETFs for long or short leverage on SNDK

But along with MU shares surging, so are those of other memory high-flyers. Sandisk (NASDAQ:SNDK) stock is up by 16% in pre-market trading, with the company set to benefit from the extremely tight conditions and pricing trends in the NAND industry.

Accordingly, Citi’sAsiya Merchant, an analyst ranked in 7th spot among the thousands of Street stock experts, has rejigged her SNDK model, due to “better near-term pricing outlook and better margins.”

“We revise our estimates upward as we remain constructive on extremely favorable S/D environment with continued indications of longer-term persistence, supportive of pricing, plus durable underlying data center demand,” the 5-star analyst said.

Merchant highlights that Citi global memory analyst Peter Lee projects CY26 NAND ASPs to increase +186% year-over-year on strong eSSD demand, with the market expected to remain undersupplied through CY28.

The tight production supply conditions should support pricing, with eSSDs benefiting from “extremely strong hyperscale demand” tied to generative-AI training/inferencing services, while vendors aim to improve profitability via price hikes and careful management of capex and supply.

More broadly, the shift toward agentic, continuous-inference AI is sharply increasing system performance demands, as AI agents repeatedly fetch data, run multiple inference steps, and use external tools, driving much higher data and compute intensity.

While RAG (retrieval of external information to ground outputs) and KV caching (reusing stored attention computations to speed up inference) help improve efficiency, growing context windows quickly exceed what GPU memory can handle economically. This leads to more frequent cache eviction and recomputation, increasing latency and total cost, with memory bandwidth and storage I/O becoming key constraints.

 To solve this, systems are moving toward memory tiering that extends GPU memory with high-speed SSDs. By storing KV caches and intermediate results on SSDs, they reduce redundant computation, ease GPU memory pressure, and improve inference efficiency. This lowers cost per inference, increases throughput, stabilizes latency, and allows RAG systems to scale using larger external knowledge and cached data.

“SNDK should continue to be a beneficiary of this structurally favorable environment, additionally coupled with its Bics8 qualifications, serving as a competitive moat, and with increasing mix to data center further benefiting its margins through the longer-term,” the analyst further said.

To this end, Merchant maintained a Buy rating on SNDK shares and raised her price target from $2,025 to $2,500, implying the stock will gain another 31% over the coming months. (To watch Merchant’s track record, click here)

Merchant has also opened an “Upside 90-Day ST View” on SNDK shares, supported by positive industry results and commentary, alongside the upcoming catalyst of an August investor day, where Merchant expects to hear “updated commentary on demand expectations, technology roadmap and constructive capital returns outlook.”

On the Street, 13 other analysts are also SNDK bulls, while 2 Holds can’t detract from a Strong Buy consensus rating. However, the $1,873.13 average price target suggests the stock is now fully valued. That likely reflects the speed of the stock’s rally more than any weakening in conviction, suggesting more upward revisions to price targets could follow shortly. (See SNDK stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘It’s Not What You Think,’ Says Stifel About Blackberry Stock
data.description:
BlackBerry (NYSE:BB) has been turning heads this year, with the stock one of the market’s most impressive performers, climbing by 133% in 2026.

TipRanks' Prime Week Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock powerful investing tools and data-driven insights with TipRanks Premium for more confident investment decisions.
  • Discover top stock picks and new investment opportunities through TipRanks' Smart Investor Newsletter.

For a name long seen as a turnaround story transitioning away from smartphones that had yet to truly play out, that process now appears to be complete, as the company has moved into a new growth phase centred on its QNX software platform.

However, despite the stock’s big move, Stifel analyst Suthan Sukumar believes the market “still misdefines” BlackBerry.

“This is no longer an auto-supplier story, but rather a mission-critical software layer in the physical AI stack and a dominant partner to silicon leaders like NVIDIA, Qualcomm, and AMD powering the build-out from cloud to edge, across cars, robots, factories, and medical devices,” the analyst said.

Sukumar sees a shift toward higher-quality revenue and earnings that resembles ARM’s capital-light royalty-based annuity model. The argument is not for an ARM-like multiple, but rather that reduced revenue risk, structurally improving margins as the mix tilts toward runtime royalties, and strong free cash flow conversion justify a premium valuation versus a “rapidly expanding” but still underpenetrated TAM (total addressable market).

“With BlackBerry in a leading competitive position, we see meaningful value to capture as the physical AI market unfolds, driving sustained acceleration in growth and margin expansion, with potential for execution upside and guidance raises,” the analyst further added.

In contrast to the “probabilistic AI” layer above it, the underlying control layer in physical systems must remain fail-safe, and QNX has functioned as that deterministic, safety-certified foundation for 40 years. Sukumar says that his due diligence across silicon partners and distributors also supports the view that there is no alternative that matches QNX’s blend of safety certification and real-time performance at scale.

Certifications spanning multiple verticals, a distinct microkernel design, and a “silicon flywheel” with Nvidia, Qualcomm, and Arm together form “hard-to-replicate barriers” across automotive, industrial, and medical domains. A record and still-growing ~$950 million royalty backlog in high-margin runtime royalties underpins multi-year visibility that few peers can replicate, even amid subdued auto production.

Meanwhile, the software-defined stack underpinning QNX’s automotive strength is now extending into factories, hospitals, and robotics, with non-automotive general embedded markets (GEM) already accounting for ~20% of QNX revenue and converting faster than automotive programmes (6–12 months vs. ~5 years).

Expansion up the stack into Alloy Kore middleware (a higher-level software layer that sits between the operating system and applications) could increase per-vehicle content by 3–6x, with the key catalyst being the first OEM design win, as discussions with Mercedes-Benz and other OEMs progress. Adjacent offerings such as QNX Sound and QNX Cabin further raise content per vehicle, while recent GEM wins, including a Johnson & Johnson AI heart pump, “validate the cross-vertical thesis.”

The opportunity appears to be a big one, with Sukumar’s proprietary TAM framework indicating a potential ~10x increase in QNX revenue through the 2030s.

As such, Sukumar initiated coverage of BB stock with a Buy rating and Street-high price target of $12, implying the stock will gain 36% in the months ahead. (To watch Sukumar’s track record, click here)

One other analyst joins Sukumar in the bull camp, while 3 additional Holds all add up to a Moderate Buy consensus view. However, the $7.18 average price target now sits 19% above the current share price. With this in mind, watch out for some analyst model changes shortly. (See BB stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Tesla Stock: Q2 Deliveries in Focus, but Do They Matter? UBS Weighs In
data.description:
In all the hoopla surrounding the recent SpaceX IPO, attention has been diverted away from Elon Musk’s other public concern, that little-known EV maker Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA).

TipRanks' Prime Week Sale - 70% Off

Explore TSLQ for 2X short leverage on TSLA

However, with the second quarter now in its final innings, the company will soon be under the microscope again when it announces its quarterly delivery haul.

That will take place early next month, and UBS analyst Joseph Spak expects 405,000 Q2 deliveries, representing a 5% year-over-year increase and 13% quarter-over-quarter growth. This is above his prior 2Q26 estimate of 380,000 and slightly above Visible Alpha consensus at 402,000. “We believe buyside expectations range of 400-420k. So, we are towards the lower end but concede there could be a little of upward bias if Tesla closes the quarter strong,” the analyst went on to say.

The revised 2Q26 outlook reflects softer US BEV demand alongside the planned wind-down of Model S and Model X production, with this likely being the final quarter of deliveries for those models. Based on Autodata estimates, Tesla delivered 78,100 vehicles in April and May, which is -16% vs. the first two months’ pace in 2Q25 and -1% vs. the same period in 1Q26.

In Europe, deliveries across the top eight markets are up around 99% YoY for the first two months of the quarter, benefiting from easier comps. Quarter-to-date strength is particularly evident in Germany (+294%), France (+373%), the UK (+49%), and Spain (+46%), while Italy is weaker at -17% YoY. Spak expects European deliveries to rise around 60% YoY in Q2, although performance in Europe typically skews toward the third month, leaving room for additional upside.

In China, factory output (wholesale shipments) is up +38% YoY in April and May. Domestic retail sales are up around +9% YoY through the first two months, while exports have increased by 75%, supporting demand and deliveries in other regions, including South Korea and Australia, where early-quarter demand has been solid. “As such,” says Spak, “we expect solid growth in regions outside of US/Europe/China.”

The thing is, given how the narrative around Tesla has moved away from EVs, Spak wonders whether the delivery numbers even matter for the stock price. “Probably not,” he says, “at least not like it used to.”

Spak notes that investor discussions around the traditional auto business and underlying fundamentals are “very low.” The stock price is primarily driven by narrative and longer-term optionality tied to AI-related initiatives, including Robotaxi, Optimus, Terafab, solar, as well as the “potential future status” of Tesla.

Against that backdrop, Spak believes progress in robotaxi development and scaling has been slower than bullish expectations. Musk has also previously indicated that Optimus V3 would be ready for demonstration around mid-year, a timeline that is now approaching, which could increase expectations for further detail and updates on the programme.

“While we expect sentiment will continue to overwhelmingly drive the stock (certainly more than auto deliveries), it is (primarily) the auto business that helps fund Tesla’s cash flow and hence their investment for growth ($25bn capex guidance this year, that we assume remains over coming years),” Spak summed up.

To this end, Spak maintained his Neutral rating on the stock alongside a $364 price target, implying shares will decline by 5% over the coming months. (To watch Spak’s track record, click here)

14 other analysts join Spak on the sidelines, while an additional 11 Buys and 3 Sells all add up to a Moderate Buy consensus rating. The forecast calls for 12-month returns of 6%, considering the average target clocks in at $403.49. (See Tesla stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Cerebras Stock’s First Earnings Are About to Hit – Here’s What Morgan Stanley Expects
data.description:
Investors are waiting in anticipation for Micron’s earnings tomorrow (Wednesday), but before that big event takes place, the new chipmaker on the block will hog the limelight.

TipRanks' Prime Week Sale - 70% Off

Two ETFs for long or short leverage on CBRS

Once the market action comes to a halt today, Cerebras Systems (NASDAQ:CBRS) will deliver its Q1 earnings report, its first quarterly statement since going public on May 14.

Cerebras differentiates itself from other chipmakers through its unique product design. It builds AI systems around a single giant wafer-scale processor that is far larger than conventional chips, allowing more computation and memory to stay on one device and reducing the need for complex multi-chip communication.

Looking ahead to the print, Morgan Stanley’s Joseph Moore, an analyst ranked among the top 2% on Wall Street, is not expecting a “major surprise,” although he remains constructive on what he sees as a “differentiated architecture with significant upside.”

With substantial take-or-pay contracts already secured, Moore views the near-term narrative as being driven by execution rather than demand. The analyst continues to see the rate of capacity rollout as the “key driver” influencing upside to revenue and gross margins and remains focused on management’s progress toward bringing the initial 250MW tranche online, which he currently expects by mid-2027, as part of a phased 750MW contracted compute deployment with OpenAI.

Longer term, Moore considers the key question to be whether the Wafer Scale Engine can establish itself as a “durable competitive advantage.” The company will need to show that the technology offers “meaningful value” and is able to scale in an economically viable way.  

As for the numbers, Moore is calling for core revenue of $180 million and core gross margin of 44% for the March quarter, broadly in line with consensus expectations. Looking ahead, he anticipates core revenue to stay “roughly flat” in the June quarter while core gross margin drops to 24%, on account of G42 rent-back arrangements that support the initial capacity ramp. He expects these arrangements to continue weighing on cloud margins until around mid-2027.

On a GAAP basis, Moore expects revenue of $182 million in the March quarter and $153 million in June, with the gap between GAAP and core revenue largely reflecting warrant-related contra revenue.

While the near-term compute ramp may remain “somewhat messy,” accompanied by margin pressure and warrant-related expenses, the analyst believes these challenges are “well understood” by investors.

“Ultimately,” the 5-star analyst summed up, “what we are playing for is a differentiated architecture with leadership in one of the fastest growing segments of AI infrastructure, where successful execution could unlock significant upside beyond current expectations.”

To this end, Moore reiterated an Overweight (i.e., Buy) rating on CBRS shares alongside a $250 price target, implying the stock will gain 12% in the months ahead. (To watch Moore’s track record, click here)

This is a stock that gets the Street’s full support. Based on a unanimous 10 Buys, the analyst consensus naturally rates the shares a Strong Buy. The average target clocks in at $294, implying shares will gain 32% in the months ahead. (See CBRS stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘Buy the Dip,’ Says Daniel Ives Following South Korea-Driven Tech Stocks Rout
data.description:
Tuesday’s premarket is looking like a bit of a bloodbath for tech stocks. Futures tied to the Nasdaq (NDX) dropped sharply amid rising scepticism around the AI trade, leaving the tech-heavy index lower by 2.83%, while the S&P 500 (SPX) declined by 1.45%.

TipRanks' Prime Week Sale - 70% Off

  • Unlock powerful investing tools and data-driven insights with TipRanks Premium for more confident investment decisions.
  • Discover top stock picks and new investment opportunities through TipRanks' Smart Investor Newsletter.

The broader decline reflects a sharp sell-off in South Korean memory chip leaders SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics, highlighting intensifying concerns over rich AI valuations.

Each of the two companies saw their shares fall more than 12%, pulling the benchmark KOSPI index down by 10% and triggering trading circuit breakers during the session.

The KOSPI’s meltdown comes a day after SK Hynix (HXSCL) surpassed Samsung (SSNLF) as South Korea’s most valuable listed company, while the index reached its historic 9,000 level late last week.

Wading in on the situation, Wedbush analyst and tech bull Daniel Ives thinks it is only logical that the sell-off will cause “selling pressure and white knuckles” for US tech stocks this morning, as investors fret that the overheated KOSPI downturn could spill over into US tech names.

The analyst notes that with the KOSPI up roughly 90% year-to-date, it has formed the foundation of the AI boom, with memory names such as SK Hynix, Micron (NASDAQ:MU), and Samsung acting as the “golden jewels of this demand-driven AI Revolution rally” seen over the past 18 months in South Korea.

At the same time, ahead of Micron’s highly-anticipated Wednesday earnings report, Ives thinks there is “some added nervousness” around the key memory chip trade, and alongside technical factors this is increasing anxiety among technology investors.

Yet, zooming out, the analyst maintains that this market will continue to experience multiple “gut check moments” in tech trading as the AI Revolution remains in its 3rd inning, and this morning’s price action is “just another one of those moments.”

Meanwhile, Ives notes that his latest Asia channel checks and broader AI enterprise demand tracking have accelerated over recent months and show “no cracks in the armor,” reinforcing his very bullish stance on owning “tech AI winners” over the next year.

And in time-honored fashion, Ives believes the sell-off represents an opportunity investors should make the most of. “We view the KOSPI sell-off as a pullback/breather on a market up almost 100% this year and also believe SK overtaking Samsung was a big symbolic move that caused some investors to worry about an ‘overheated’ memory chip trade…..which we strongly disagree with as earnings/demand/scarcity value will rule the day in this multi-trillion AI global buildout over the coming years,” the 5-star analyst summed up. (To watch Ives’ track record, click here)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘This Is the Change That Matters,’ Says Morgan Stanley About Sandisk Stock
data.description:
AI is reshaping the dynamics of the NAND industry, as its biggest end market has become considerably less sensitive to pricing changes than the PC and mobile segments.

TipRanks' Prime Week Sale - 70% Off

High conviction on SNDK? Try Tradr's SNXX or SNDQ

That is the conclusion reached by Morgan Stanley’s Joseph Moore, an analyst ranked among the top 2% on Wall Street, following recent investor meetings with Sandisk’s (NASDAQ:SNDK) head of investor relations, Ivan Donaldson. “The company remains upbeat on the outlook for the NAND industry and Sandisk specific drivers, with no line of sight to a balance in industry supply/demand,” the 5-star analyst further said.

Moore points out that NAND is experiencing a “fundamental repricing” as demand from AI inference workloads becomes a more direct driver of the market. Broadly speaking, inference-related requirements are generating significant demand for NAND technologies to “move up in the memory hierarchy,” since the expanding storage needs associated with LLM KV caches and larger context windows cannot be satisfied solely through DRAM.

This represents a “very different value proposition” in the datacenter from the cost-per-bit improvements that some believed would be the primary catalyst for replacing HDDs, although Sandisk has consistently maintained that the two technologies serve complementary roles. As cloud is expected to overtake other segments and become the largest end market for NAND later this year, overall industry dynamics are changing, given that cloud customer demand is far less dependent on NAND pricing than demand from PC and mobile markets.

Meanwhile, more than one-third of Sandisk’s FY27 bit output has been committed under NBM (New Business Model) agreements, which the company believes introduces a “new level of durability” to the business.

With the NAND supply/demand balance remaining “very tight,” Moore anticipates ongoing ASP hikes through CY26 and possibly extending well into 2027. Right now, the analyst factors in a degree of ASP decline beginning around mid-2027 and thereafter, which is actually “not in evidence today,” and with more than one-third of Sandisk’s business already contracted at 80%+ gross margins that far into the future, the “range of outcomes should be skewed to the upside.”

That said, Moore does not think 80% margins are a “new normal” for NAND, and he interprets that level as “emblematic of a commodity in a shortage,” although he still agrees that NAND’s enhanced value contribution in a world less centered on PC and mobile should support a “higher level of trough economics than past cycles.”

“As long as datacenter markets continue to grow at a pace well above NAND industry bit supply, we think suppliers will retain pricing power, and that shapes our positive view on the stock,” Moore summed up.

As such, Moore maintained an Overweight rating on SNDK shares. However, his $1,750 price target remains as is for now, and sits 23% below the current share price. (To watch Moore’s track record, click here)

It’s a similar story elsewhere on Wall Street. On the one hand, the stock claims a Strong Buy consensus rating, based on a mix of 14 Buys and 2 Holds. However, the $1,843.44 average price target implies the stock will decline by 19% in the months ahead. That gap likely reflects more the speed of the stock’s recent rally than any weakening in conviction, suggesting upward revisions to price targets could soon follow. (See Sandisk stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Why SpaceX Stock Is Falling Today and Why the Sidelines Beckon for Now
data.description:
SpaceX (NASDAQ:SPCX) shares’ pullback looks set to continue on Monday, with a new Wall Street research note appearing to be the culprit.  

TipRanks' Prime Week Sale - 70% Off

High conviction SPCX bulls now have this Tradr ETF

KeyBanc analyst Michael Leshock initiated coverage of the stock with a Sector Weight (i.e., Neutral) rating without offering a fixed price target. (To watch Leshock’s track record, click here)

It’s not that Leshock thinks that the Elon Musk-led firm is a bad company in any way. In fact, Leshock sees plenty to like here. “SPCX is positioned to remain the dominant leader in space launch and space-adjacent verticals for the foreseeable future, capitalizing on its head start vs. the competition,” the 5-star analyst said. “The vertically integrated nature of the business and owning launch vehicles allow SPCX to control its destiny with the myriad longer-term growth aspirations across unprecedented TAMs.”

Leshock calls Starlink its “crown jewel.” It serves as the “primary revenue driver,” overtaking Launch in 2023, and is “uniquely positioned” for growth due to its unmatched space-based distribution that extends beyond terrestrial constraints. Leshock sees it as having “significant long-term upside,” especially as performance metrics such as speed, latency, capacity, and reliability continue to advance, enabling access to densely populated urban markets. Additionally, robust “incremental margins” and expanding EBITDA could see further uplift over time, supported by declining capital intensity through full launch vehicle reusability. “While competition will emerge, we believe Starlink’s first-mover advantage, performance roadmap (V3), and global reach position it as the clear leader in next-gen connectivity,” Leshock further said.

Meanwhile, AI and data center expansion ambitions open up a vast TAM (total addressable market). The company estimates the TAM for AI-related applications at $26.5 trillion, equivalent to approximately 20% of global GDP. While this TAM figure is difficult to validate with any certainty, Leshock does believe that there is an “unprecedented level of opportunity ahead,” with AI-related end-use cases expected to evolve significantly as the industry matures. Recent deals exceeding $10 billion annually with Anthropic and Google indicate that AI-driven growth may arrive sooner than anticipated and should remain the primary driver of the overall business for the foreseeable future.

The thing is, many of the “significant long-term growth avenues” rely on Starship – the company’s fully reusable super-heavy launch system – achieving success. Starship is regarded as a breakthrough in engineering, but it is still in development and has already encountered certain setbacks that have delayed its original schedule by several years. Such delays might be typical across any nascent launch vehicle program, and Leshock does expect Starship to eventually succeed, but for now he adopts a cautious stance regarding its development timeframe.

So, while SpaceX has substantial “disruptive growth” pathways, Leshock believes these are already largely embedded in the current valuation, leaving the risk-reward profile balanced right now.

That’s the KeyBanc view, but what does the rest of the Street have in mind for SPCX stock? Based on an additional 3 Buys and 1 Sell, the stock claims a Moderate Buy consensus rating. At $235.25, the average price target points toward 12-month returns of 27%. (See SPCX stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: Bank of America Lays Out Expectations Ahead of Amazon Stock’s Prime Day
data.description:
Amazon’s (NASDAQ:AMZN) 12th annual Prime Day is scheduled for next week, taking place between June 23-26, and keeping last year’s 96-hour structure while shifting forward from July.  

Claim 55% Off TipRanks

High conviction AMZN bears now have this Tradr ETF

The event is set to offer “millions” of promotions across more than 35 categories, spanning electronics, apparel, beauty, and other segments. Amazon is emphasizing discounts on its proprietary devices, including Alexa+-enabled products (with savings of up to 60%) and Kindles (with discounts of up to 45%), alongside offers in categories with lower penetration rates such as grocery and travel.

Amazon is also promoting its AI-powered shopping assistant, Alexa for Shopping (AfS), as an important resource for product discovery and deal monitoring throughout Prime Day. The company states that AfS is capable of generating personalized offers and product suggestions based on an individual shopper’s purchase history and preferences.

BofA’s Justin Post, an analyst ranked among the top 2% on Wall Street, thinks that AfS will be an “essential tool in protecting direct traffic” for Amazon, while also supporting improved conversion rates and encouraging “incremental spend” across the platform. In total, the analyst estimates that AfS could contribute more than $200 billion in incremental GMV by 2035 and generate $20 billion in additional Retail profit.

As Prime Day occurs in June this year rather than July as it did in 2025, this will affect year-over-year quarterly comparisons. Adjusting for timing differences, Post reckons 1P Prime Day GMV will increase 4.5% YoY to $11.6 billion, while 3P GMV is expected to rise 5% YoY to $10 billion. Combined, the analyst forecasts Prime Day GMV of $21.6 billion, representing growth of about 5%, compared with the 55% increase recorded in 2025 that benefited from the expansion to a 96-hour event. Post estimates that Prime Day could contribute $12.4 billion in incremental Q2 GMV relative to normal sales levels for those dates, along with $8.5 billion in incremental revenue.

Overall, based on strong retail sales trends through May, the analyst believes Amazon remains positioned to achieve results at or above the upper end of its $199 billion Q2 revenue guide, although Q3 will face a significant Prime Day comparison headwind.

While competing retailers (Walmart and Target) are also moving their sales events earlier, Post expects Amazon to continue gaining share within online retail, with Prime Day highlighting the benefits of Prime membership through factors such as pricing, selection, and rapid delivery. This year also marks the first Prime Day features AfS branding, which Post expects Amazon will market aggressively in order to increase awareness and accelerate user engagement.

“While margins are always a risk during heavy promotion periods, we believe Amazon is well positioned to offset pressures through continued efficiency gains in its logistics network & a potential uplift to advertising during the event,” the 5-star analyst summed up.

All told, Post reiterated a Buy rating on AMZN shares, while his $310 price objective makes room for 12-month returns of 27%. (To watch Post’s track record, click here)

Almost all other analysts agree with that stance. Based on an additional 44 Buys vs. 1 lone Hold, the stock claims a Strong Buy consensus rating. The forecast calls for 12-month returns of 30.5%, considering the average target clocks in at $319.14. (See Amazon stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘Everyone Wins’: Oppenheimer Sizes up SpaceX Stock’s Cursor Deal
data.description:
Earlier this week SpaceX (NASDAQ:SPCX) announced that it will buy AI startup Cursor for $60 billion in an all-stock transaction.

Claim 55% Off TipRanks

High conviction on SPCX? Try Tradr's SPCM or SPCG

The company anticipates the deal will be finalized in the third quarter, subject to regulatory approvals. The deal comes after an option SpaceX obtained in April, granting it the choice to either spend approximately $10 billion on a partnership with Cursor or proceed with a $60 billion acquisition later in the year.

Cursor is an AI-driven code editor built to speed up software engineering workflows. It incorporates a chatbot assistant, predictive code completion, and autonomous AI agents capable of executing coding tasks independently, alongside additional functionalities.

Oppenheimer analyst Timothy Horan thinks the pair make a perfect match, given both companies need each other to realize their respective ambitions.  

“Agentic coding is one of the first killer Apps of AI,” says Horan, and Cursor stands as a category leader, while being the “fastest-scaling company on record.” It has delivered the most rapid SaaS expansion to date, rising from $100 million ARR in January 2025 to approximately $4 billion at present, with over 1 million daily active users, around 64% penetration across the Fortune 500, and roughly a 60% enterprise revenue mix. Its customer base includes Nvidia, Uber, Stripe, and OpenAI. On the downside, Cursor has been operating with negative gross margins, as inference costs – its largest expense – have been procured at retail pricing from Anthropic and OpenAI, both of which are direct competitors.

Its core advantage lies in context and orchestration design – specifically how it retrieves, prioritizes, and supplies codebase context, executes multi-agent workflows, and integrates these systems into a high-performance IDE, effectively wrapping the entire LLM stack in developer tooling. Frontier models tend to deliver stronger performance when run within Cursor compared with direct API usage.

However, this edge could prove temporary without substantial compute scale, as competing models continue to improve, and without the merger, Cursor risks being bypassed by the very model providers it currently resells.

As such, Cursor receives compute to train and run inference on its models, while in exchange SpaceX obtains orchestration engineering, proprietary data, and a locked-in base of advanced developers – strengthening its AI flywheel and enabling vertical integration that supports both innovation and margin expansion.

So, a win-win for everyone. “This deal is highly beneficial for both sides–Cursor needs the compute and SPCX needs the product and userbase. On close, we will be monitoring the integration with xAI infrastructure, the training of a competitive new model, and continued userbase and revenue growth, which are critical for the SPCX AI story and will be key catalysts to derisk the stock,” Horan summed up.

Conveying his confidence, Horan reiterated an Outperform (i.e., Buy) rating on SPCX shares, and separately also raised his price target from $190 to $250, implying the shares will gain 35% in the year ahead. (To watch Horan’s track record, click here)

2 other analysts also rate the stock a Buy, while 1 additional Hold makes the consensus view a Moderate Buy. Over the next year shares are expected to appreciate by 27%, considering the average price target clocks in at $235.25. (See SPCX stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel

data.title: ‘This Is the Opportunity’: Marvell Stock Gets a New Street-High Price Target
data.description:
Marvell (NASDAQ:MRVL) shares are surging by 10% in Thursday’s session after the high-flying chip stock gained a new Street-high price target.

Claim 55% Off TipRanks

  • Unlock powerful investing tools and data-driven insights with TipRanks Premium for more confident investment decisions.
  • Discover top stock picks and new investment opportunities through TipRanks' Smart Investor Newsletter.

KeyBanc’s John Vinh, an analyst ranked among the top 1% of Street stock experts, raised his target from $260 to $385, implying shares will gain another 21% in the months ahead. Vinh’s rating stays an Overweight (i.e., Buy). (To watch Vinh’s track record, click here)

The 5-star analyst views networking as the most “significant and durable” source of data center growth for Marvell over the coming years. Vinh estimates the networking business could help drive data center revenue above $20 billion by FY29.

A central part of this outlook is the scale-up (SU) networking market, which connects AI accelerators within large computing clusters. The analyst estimates this greenfield market will reach $30 billion by 2030, excluding Nvidia, consisting of roughly $20 billion in optical networking and $10 billion in switching.

Based on his assumptions that Marvell captures 50% share in optical and 30% share in switching, Vinh reckons the company could generate ~$13 billion in scale-up revenue by 2030.

He also highlights Marvell’s investments in both ESUN and UALink, while noting that Broadcom is focused primarily on ESUN. Additionally, the analyst points to a significant increase in scale-up content, driven by SerDes bandwidth expanding 40-fold to 64 Tbps and XPU density per rack rising from 72 to 576 over the next several years. He believes the shift toward capturing full-module content rather than pluggable components could increase ASPs to roughly $1,000, more than offsetting the loss of approximately $100 in DSP content.

Beyond that, scale-out (SO) networking, which connects AI clusters or racks across a data center, is expected to continue delivering “outsized growth,” with revenue doubling this year to roughly $5 billion as the industry transitions to 1.6T connectivity. Vinh also projects scale-across (SA) networking, which links AI systems across different data center sites, to increase from $500 million in FY26 to $1 billion in FY28, an area where he believes Marvell “remains dominant.”

While Vinh views custom XPUs as less durable over the long term than networking, he still sees a clear path for that business to reach $10 billion in revenue by FY29. He believes Marvell has strong visibility into upcoming ramps at AWS and Microsoft, although management has acknowledged that AWS will use multiple suppliers, including Alchip. He also notes that visibility into follow-on deployments at Microsoft is less certain, given that Maia 300 is effectively a first-generation AI ASIC, unlike Google’s TPU program, which is already entering its seventh generation.

For FY29, the analyst expects custom XPU revenue to be driven by three primary factors: $2 billion to $3 billion from Microsoft’s Maia 300 program, which he considers conservative given projected lifetime volumes of roughly 300,000 units and ASPs of $12,000 to $13,000; another $2 billion to $3 billion from AWS Trainium; and the remaining contribution from approximately 12 XPU attach opportunities tied to technologies such as CXL, SmartNICs, and security offload solutions.

Turning now to the general Street view, where 23 other analysts join Vinh in the bull camp, while 4 Holds can’t detract from a Strong Buy consensus rating. However, the $256.81 average price target implies the shares are overvalued by 19%. The divergence likely reflects the speed of the stock’s recent rally (up 274% year-to-date) more than any deterioration in conviction, suggesting that more upward revisions to price targets could soon follow. (See MRVL stock forecast)
data.author: Marty Shtrubel
